data.title: DeSantis: ‘Big pool’ of Trump voters an issue for GOP in midterms
data.description:
Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) said President Trump’s voters may be an issue for the Republican Party during next year’s midterms because this “big pool” of people may not show up to vote without him on the ballot. 

“I think Republicans have an issue that Donald Trump has created a big pool of voters, but some of them are unique to him, right?” DeSantis said Friday on “Fox & Friends.” “So they’ll go vote for Trump, and they’ll vote for all Republicans when Trump is on the ballot. But if he’s not on the ballot, some of them don’t vote.”

He said that this dynamic played out during every midterm election.

“In an off-year midterm, the party in power’s voters tend to be more complacent,” he said. “The party out of power, they get upset, right? That’s just the nature of midterms.”

He cautioned Republicans against thinking that they would win districts that voted for Trump last year, saying these voters had come out for the president and might not for other GOP members running for office.

“You can overcome that,” DeSantis said, pointing to Republican wins in Florida during the 2022 elections. 

DeSantis won his reelection as governor in a landslide victory in 2022, while Republicans statewide flipped four Democratic-held House districts, bringing the number of Florida House Republicans up to 20. The state has 28 House seats. 

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who then served as a senator, also won reelection against Democratic opponent Val Demmings. 

“We won by a million and a half votes, 20 points, the biggest Republican win ever,” DeSantis said. “But we did that based on results and substance. We did it by flying under a banner of bold colors, not pale pastels.

“We were sticking it to the left every single day and delivering big victories,” he continued. “So, it can be done.”

data.title: Florida’s ‘Alligator Alcatraz’ ‘box’ may amount to torture, says Amnesty International
data.description:
Amnesty International on Thursday said the use of a confined space called the “box” at the migrant detention facility in Florida known as Alligator Alcatraz could amount to torture.

The human rights organization said in a 61-page digital report that the box, a 2-by-2-foot cage located in Alligator Alcatraz’s yard, keeps people restrained to the ground and holds them still for hours. Exposure to the heat, insects and the elements puts these prisoners at risk, Amnesty stated.

“The conditions of the ‘box’ … would likely cause severe physical and psychological pain and suffering,” the report read. “Amnesty International considers that the use of the ‘box’ as a form of punishment at ‘Alligator Alcatraz’ constitutes torture.”

Four men who said they were subject to punishment in the cage told Amnesty about its conditions. Amnesty made these and other findings during a trip to the detention facility in September.

The Hill reached out to the White House and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for comment.

Amnesty’s report also concluded that people there were held in “inhuman and unsanitary conditions, including overflowing toilets with fecal matter seeping into where people are sleeping, limited access to showers, exposure to insects without protective measures, lights on 24 hours a day, poor quality food and water, and lack of privacy.”

“Amnesty International also documented the routine and prolonged use of shackles on individuals detained for immigration purposes, both at detention facilities and during transfer between facilities, which constitutes cruel, inhuman and degrading treatment, and may amount to torture or other ill-treatment,” the report stated.

Along with poor conditions, Amnesty noted overcrowding, delays in intake procedures, inadequate and inaccessible medical care and the practice of prolonged solitary confinement. The use of prolonged solitary confinement at “the facility “constitute cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment, and may amount to torture or other ill-treatment,” the report read.

Based on the report’s findings, Amnesty called on the federal government to stop criminalizing immigrants, ban the use of state-owned facilities for immigration custody detention; follow thorough investigations into all deaths, abuses and allegations of torture in custody; and follow international human rights laws and standards.

An appeals court previously paused the dismantling of Alligator Alcatraz after a lower court ordered it be taken down in September. The lower court deemed the facility to be a threat to the environment.

data.title: Survivors of boat strike did not radio for backup, Bradley tells lawmakers: Report
data.description:
The two survivors of the early September boat attack by the U.S. military did not seem to radio for backup or appear to have other communication devices available after their vessel was struck, the top official overseeing the Caribbean operation told lawmakers during Thursday’s classified, closed-door briefings, CNN reported, citing three sources familiar with the matter. 

Adm. Frank Bradley, who is the commander of U.S. Special Operations Command (SoCom), told lawmakers on Capitol Hill that the two survivors of the Sept. 2 attack were not positioned to execute a distress call, the outlet reported. 

The Hill has reached out to SoCom for comment. 

Bradley, a decorated Navy admiral, was at the time of the attack against the alleged drug-trafficking boat — the first lethal U.S. military operation since the counternarcotics campaign kicked off in the region — heading the Joint Special Operations Command and ordered the second strike after the first one killed nine “narco-terrorists.” 

Bradley’s reported acknowledgment to lawmakers during the briefings comes as military officials have argued that the killing of the remaining survivors did not constitute a war crime because, in part, the alleged drug-smugglers seem to have tried to use the radio to call for backup or assistance. If secured, this would, in theory, permit them to resume their drug-trafficking on the capsized vessel.

After the initial strike, which killed most of those aboard and split the vessel in half, U.S. forces, at the direction of Bradley, conducted three more hits, the second one killing the last two survivors. The third and fourth strikes sank the boat, Sen. Chris Coons (D-Del.), who is on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, told reporters Thursday after the briefing. 

Following the first strike, the two survivors attempted to flip the boat and continue on with the mission, Sen. Tom Cotton (R-Ark.), the chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, told reporters. 

Rep. Adam Smith (D-Wash.), the ranking member on the House Armed Services Committee, while appearing Thursday on NewsNation’s “The Hill,” disagreed with Cotton’s characterization of events and said the two survivors were leaning on the bow of the boat, the only part of the vessel that was still sticking out of the water after the first strike. 

“There was no way they were flipping that back over and going anywhere. The boat was totally disabled and floating in the current,” Smith said Thursday evening. 

Smith said that part of the reason Bradley authorized the second strike was that U.S. military officials could not tell if all of the drugs on board, likely cocaine, were destroyed. 

“But that was the basis for the strike, not that they were trying to right-size the boat and go on their merry way. That wasn’t happening. It was, I suppose, arguably possible that someone could come to assist them, but we had eyes on the scene, and nobody was,” the Washington Democrat told host Blake Burman. 

When asked Friday morning on CNN if he saw evidence during the briefing that the two survivors tried to use a radio, Cotton said “no,” but emphasized that the alleged drug-smugglers were “clearly not incapacitated.” 

The Arkansas senator said the survivors were not “distressed” and that one of them took his t-shirt off after the strike as he was “sunbathing.” 

“They were trying to get the boat back up and to continue their mission of spreading these drugs all across America,” Cotton said. 

Following the briefings on Thursday, Democrats said they were disturbed after watching the video of the Sept. 2 operation, with Rep. Jim Himes (Conn.), the top Democrat on the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence, saying the evidence indicated that the U.S. troops targeted the two survivors who did not pose a threat to U.S. security. 

“I reviewed the video, and it’s deeply, deeply troubling,” Himes said on Thursday. “The fact is that we killed two people who were in deep distress and had neither the means nor obviously the intent to continue their mission.”

House Intelligence Chair Rick Crawford (R-Ark.) agreed with Cotton’s characterization of events, saying the survivors tried to flip back the vessel that still contained illicit narcotics since the drugs were “strapped into” it. 

“They were potentially signaling other vessels in the region,” Crawford said during his appearance on Fox News, adding that “we don’t know if they had access to SATCOMs or not, but as he characterized, still in the fight.”

data.title: Vanity Fair parts ways with Olivia Nuzzi after book release
data.description:
Vanity Fair will part ways with journalist Olivia Nuzzi, the reporter who wrote in a new book about a romantic relationship she says she had with Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., according to a new report.

Nuzzi’s contract as a West Coast editor at VF was set to expire at the end of the year, the Wall Street Journal reported, noting the two sides have agreed to part ways.

A former top political reporter for The Daily Beast and New York Magazine, Nuzzi was fired by the magazine after it was revealed she had a romantic relationship with Kennedy, which the HHS secretary denies.

Nuzzi published a new memoir on Tuesday, in which she writes extensively about how she got close with Kennedy as she profiled him during the 2024 race for president.

“American Canto,” has been widely panned by mainstream media critics, including a Washington Post review which argued the book “tries and fails to save her reputation.”

data.title: Vance lists the three progressive politicians he now appreciates
data.description:
Vice President Vance shared a list of the three progressive politicians who he said, in an interview with NBC News published Thursday, he has come to appreciate.

In a wide-ranging interview that touched on his marriage, his chances of running in 2028 and antisemitism in the United States, Vance said he has come to appreciate Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) and New York City Mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani (D).

Within the context of the interview, Vance said that there are no Democrats who worry him as possibly strong contenders in 2028. He then brought up Sanders, Khanna and Mamdani.

“I’ve always been fascinated by Bernie,” Vance said. “I will one day tell you what Bernie said to me, like the second day that I was in the United States Senate. It’s one of the funniest things that I’ve ever heard, and it’s actually a pretty good summary of my politics, but it would probably really hurt me on both the left and the right. If I told you what Bernie told me, it would probably hurt Bernie, too.”

Vance said he has sparred with Khanna on social media. The vice president added that while he at times thinks Khanna is “very annoying … he occasionally will say something interesting, which is more than I could say for most politicians.”

Echoing President Trump, Vance said he thinks Mamdani, a democratic socialist, is a communist. Despite this, he credited him for focusing “so aggressively on the affordability question in New York City, which does have one of the worst affordability crises anywhere in the world, is smart, and he’s at least listening to people.”

Vance also cited Trump’s meeting with Mamdani last month, where the president praised the New York Democrat. After he met with him, Vance called Mamdani “fascinating.”

“Most politicians, it’s a very low bar, but they don’t even listen to people,” Vance added. “I would put Mamdani, Bernie and Ro Khanna in the category of those who, at least sometimes, they are.”

Despite his praise for the three, particularly Mamdani’s focus on affordability, Vance told NBC News that the concern over the issue is part of a “fake narrative.” Trump has reversed course on affordability: instead of touting how Republicans are tackling the issue, calling the issue a “hoax “con job” manufactured by Democrats.

“I think the president certainly understands that prices got way too high,” Vance said. “But I think what the president’s saying is the idea that, 11 months into the administration, that we could solve all of the affordability problems created by Democrats — I mean, that’s the hoax. The hoax is the idea that it’s our fault and not the Democrats’ fault. And I do think that’s a totally bulls— narrative.”

With Sanders not expected to run for president again in 2028 and Mamdani two years into his role as mayor, Khanna has been speculated as a potential 2028 contender.

Vance admitted to trying to “steer away from the 2028 conversation” so that he could focus on current matters. The outcome of the 2026 midterms could define not only the rest of Trump’s second term but also Vance’s future post-vice presidency, he said.

“The president really needs a vice president who is loyal to him and doesn’t use the media to backstab or to set himself up well for 2028,” Vance told NBC News. “So what you will never hear me do in this job is attack the president of the United States.”

“Of course, if I run for another office in the future, it’s going to be reasonable for people to ask me, ‘Would you have done this thing? Would you have done that thing?’” the vice president added. “And if that time ever comes, let’s have that conversation. But I am never going to attack the president of the United States.”

data.title: Lloyd Doggett won’t seek reelection in light of Supreme Court’s Texas redistricting ruling
data.description:
Texas Rep. Lloyd Doggett (D) has announced that he will not seek reelection in wake of the Supreme Court’s decision upholding the state’s new district map. 

“I will continue working with the same urgency and determination as if next year were my last, which in public office it will be,” Doggett said in a Friday press release announcing his retirement. “After that, I will seek new ways to join my neighbors in making a difference in the only town I have ever called home.”

Texas GOP lawmakers began pushing for a new congressional map earlier this year that would change district boundaries within the state to favor Republican candidates and reduce the number of Democrat-held House seats. 

Democratic Texas lawmakers originally fled the state to prevent the state Legislature from having the quorum needed to pass its redistricting measure. 

After assurances from California Gov. Gavin Newsom and other Democrats that they would pursue redistricting in their own states to favor Democratic candidates and offset GOP House gains under Texas’s new map, they returned to Texas and the map was able to be passed into law. 

A panel of federal judges ruled in November that the new map was likely a racial gerrymander, blocking the state from using it. The case made its way to the Supreme Court, which issued an administrative stay in the case shortly after the ruling. 

The high court then ruled Thursday that Texas could use its new map, paving the way for its use in next year’s midterm elections. 

Doggett has served as a House representative since 1995. In his Friday press release, he praised Justice Elena Kagan’s dissent in the Supreme Court’s decision on his state’s redistricting. 

“Trump’s racial gerrymandering is only his first major shenanigan designed to win next year’s election and retain a House that poses no restraint to his dangerous whims and incessant drive for unlimited power,” he said. “More outrageous schemes will follow.”

“I will continue doing my part to vigorously fight back and to help others here and across the country,” he continued.

data.title: Judge orders Epstein investigation transcripts released in Florida
data.description:
A federal judge on Friday ordered the Department of Justice (DOJ) to release jury transcripts from Florida’s investigation into convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

U.S. District Judge Rodney Smith made the order in compliance with the Epstein Files Transparency Act, which requires the DOJ to release all of its information on Epstein. President Trump signed the bill into law last month following a discharge vote and passage in the House, then unanimous consent in the Senate.

The law’s passage overrode any protective orders that barred the DOJ from releasing any grand jury materials.

While it is unclear when this information will be released, the DOJ and Attorney General Pam Bondi have until Dec. 19 to start releasing the files and information on Epstein.

Smith’s order comes after lawyers for Epstein’s associate Ghislaine Maxwell said their client plans to soon file a court petition challenging her detention. If her court petition succeeds, it could result in a new trial.

But Maxwell’s lawyers argued against the release of any grand jury material in compliance with the law. They said the materials’ release “would create undue prejudice so severe that it would foreclose the possibility of a fair retrial should Ms. Maxwell’s habeas petition succeed,” lawyer David Oscar Markus wrote.

Maxwell was previously found guilty of conspiring with and aiding Epstein in the sexual abuse of underage girls. She was sentenced to 20 years in prison.

On Wednesday, Democrats on the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee on Wednesday released photos of Epstein’s home in the U.S. Virgin Islands. The photos show the word “power” written in chalk on the wall, while masks of men’s faces adorn a dental suite.

“These new images are a disturbing look into the world of Jeffrey Epstein and his island,” Rep. Robert Garcia (Calif.), the top Democrat on the panel, said in a statement. “We are releasing these photos and videos to ensure public transparency in our investigation and to help piece together the full picture of Epstein’s horrific crimes.”

Along with the release of the files, Trump ordered Bondi to launch an investigation into Epstein’s associations with prominent Democrats, including former President Bill Clinton and former Harvard University President Larry Summers.

The DOJ stated in a memo that a “systematic review” did not uncover any evidence on which charges against “uncharged third parties” could be based.

Among the documents released prior to the Epstein files law’s passage, House Democrats released emails written by Epstein that referred to Trump. At one point, Epstein wrote that Trump “knew about the girls.” Trump has denied any wrongdoing and association with Epstein.

data.title: New Democrats call on Hegseth to resign over Signal report
data.description:
The New Democrat Coalition on Friday urged Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth to resign over an unclassified inspector General report that found he endangered service members by communicating about sensitive military intelligence in a Signal group chat. 

“Time and time again, the Secretary has lied, dodged, deflected, and shockingly scapegoated his subordinates,” New Democrat Chair Brad Schneider (D-Ill.) and National Security Working Group Chair Gil Cisneros (D-Calif.) wrote in a statement following the report’s release.

“He is a disgrace to the office he holds and should resign immediately before his actions cost American lives,” he added.

Details of the chat emerged in March, when The Atlantic’s editor-in-chief was mistakenly placed in the Signal group chat where pending strikes on Houthi targets in Yemen were being discussed.

Vice President Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio and former national security adviser Mike Waltz were included in the messaging thread, where emojis were used to laud the planned attack. 

Officials past and present warned that if the detailed strike plans Hegseth shared had been intercepted by a foreign adversary, it would have clearly endangered troops. 

Throughout the investigation into the chat, Hegseth refused to sit for an interview with the inspector general team, would not provide all the Signal messages, forcing the OIG to rely on screenshots published by The Atlantic. 

The New Democrats’ push to oust Hegseth comes as he is facing growing pushback for ordering strikes on alleged “narcoterrorists” traveling in boats across the Pacific Ocean.

The White House has defended Hegseth’s orders, alleging that after drug trafficking groups were dubbed foreign terrorist organizations, lethal strikes were legal. However, some lawmakers and past military officials say the Sept. 2 attack constitutes a war crime.

“The Inspector General’s report confirms what we’ve known all along: Secretary Hegseth is incompetent, reckless, and a threat to the lives of the men and women who serve in the Armed Forces,” the New Democrats said.

“A true leader is responsible and accountable – Hegseth is no such leader. He failed to take responsibility for this shameful incident and refused to cooperate with the independent investigation. Now he is denying any accountability for the likely illegal double-tap strike in the Caribbean,” they added.

Conflicting reports have emerged over the Sept. 2 atatck, with Hegseth and the mission commander denying an order was given to “kill everybody” on board the ship, warranting a second attack on survivors clinging to wreckage. 

The New Democrats also urged the defense secretary to resign in March over the Signal group chat and again in April for “repeatedly jeopardizing classified information and sparking widespread chaos at the Pentagon.”

“Pete Hegseth recklessly and inexcusably put the lives of service members at risk. He should immediately resign or be fired, and Congress must initiate a full investigation,” Schneider wrote in a July post on X.

data.title: Former Minnesota governor warns against ‘stigmatizing’ Somalis
data.description:
Former Minnesota Gov. Tim Pawlenty criticized President Trump’s use of the word “garbage” to describe the state’s Somali population but agreed the community does play a role in a recent fraud case.

Dozens of people have been arrested in Minnesota in relation to the Feeding Our Future fraud scheme, which the Department of Justice said saw at least $1.1 million of federal funds misused.

“For those who engaged in misconduct and legal behavior, the full force of the law should come down on them,” Pawlenty said in an appearance on “NewsNation Live,” adding, “But we shouldn’t stigmatize whole groups.”

He continued, saying there is danger in “stigmatizing any ethnic group, any racial group, any religious group.”

“I don’t think we want to start having those kinds of words applied to whole groups,” the former governor said.

Minnesota is home to the country’s largest Somali community, with about 107,000 residents as of 2024, according to U.S. Census Bureau data. The bureau reports that nationally, 73 percent of Somali immigrants are naturalized U.S. citizens.

Earlier this week, Trump said he does not want Somali migrants in the U.S. because they have “destroyed Minnesota,” continuing on to characterize them — including Somali American Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) — as “garbage.”

Sitting Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) has denounced the president’s rhetoric as an “unprecedented” and said it slanders all Minnesotans.

ICE operations targeting Minnesota’s Somali population

Trump has set his sights on the North Star State for the latest round of Immigration and Customs Enforcement operations, with Somalis at the center. Last week, he also ended “Temporary Protected Status” for Somali nationals, “effective immediately.”

Several local outlets have reported federal immigration operations are underway in Minneapolis and St. Paul.

Walz wrote on social platform X: “We welcome support in investigating and prosecuting crime. But pulling a PR stunt and indiscriminately targeting immigrants is not a real solution to the problem.”

Pawlenty decried the cities’ sanctuary policies — which prevent local authorities from cooperating with federal entities, like ICE — as “stupid.”

“When you have an ICE operation in a city like Minneapolis, and the locals refuse to protect those ICE officers, it leaves the federal entities no choice but to bring in federal police and others to protect ICE,” Pawlenty said.

Walz and other top Democrats in the state have backed its policies throughout the Trump administration’s nationwide operations.

NewsNation’s Ali Bradley and Jeff Arnold contributed to this report.

data.title: Netflix, Warner Bros. deal ‘looks like an anti-monopoly’ nightmare: Warren
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) is expressing concern about Netflix’s planned acquisition of movie maker WarnerBros, saying the deal could raise major questions around antitrust and competitive imbalance.

“This deal looks like an anti-monopoly nightmare,” Warren said Friday in a statement. “A Netflix-Warner Bros. would create one massive media giant with control of close to half of the streaming market — threatening to force Americans into higher subscription prices and fewer choices over what and how they watch, while putting American workers at risk.”

The $72 billion deal, which the two companies announced after weeks of bidding, would give some 300 million Netflix subscribers on-demand access to some of Warner Bros. Discovery’s (WBD) marquee entertainment offerings.

The deal will need to be approved by President Trump’s Federal Communications Commission (FCC), which Warren and other Democrats have criticized as favoring companies which produce content that is supportive of the president and his agenda.

“Under Donald Trump, the antitrust review process has also become a cesspool of political favoritism and corruption,” Warren said. “The Justice Department must enforce our nation’s anti-monopoly laws fairly and transparently — not use the Warner Bros. deal review to invite influence-peddling and bribery.”

WBD, like many major legacy media companies, has been under increasing pressure to sell off its linear cable and movie studio assets as more Americans cut cable in favor of streaming services and online content providers.

The Massachusetts Democrat also ripped the Trump administration earlier this year after the FCC approved the merger of Paramount, the parent of CBS News, and Skydance.
